text.fullText: Steel Manufacturers Association Stands for American Steel. 1150 Connecticut Avenue, Suite 201 Washington, DC 20036 Brandon Farris Executive Vice President February 17, 2026 The Honorable Jessica Kramer Assistant Administrator, Office of Water Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, NW Washington, DC 20460 Re: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929) Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: The Steel Manufacturers Association appreciates the opportunity to provide comments in response to the Environmental Protection Agency Office of Water’s notice of proposed rulemaking, “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations.” SMA supports EPA’s proposed procedural revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 certification as improving regulatory certainty for capital-intensive, federally permitted facilities. SMA stands for American steel. Our members operate in communities across the country and range from the nation’s largest publicly traded steel producers to single-facility, family-owned businesses. Steel manufacturers generate $149.4 billion in annual economic impact, employing 87,266 men and women in community-sustaining jobs at an average annual wage of $142,730. They create the essential material that underpins American industry and national security. Steel manufacturers are investing in America’s communities, deploying $25 billion in capital here in the U.S. to modernize, expand, and construct projects. Environmental permitting is necessary for the realization of these investments, and improvements to the permitting process will reduce unnecessary project delays. Clear timelines and defined review criteria are critical for steel mills and raw material manufacturing and handling facilities that rely on predictable federal permitting schedules. The Section 401 certification process is one such example where improvements could be made to enhance certainty for permittees while ensuring environmental protection. Steel manufacturers have noted difficulties in securing authorizations under the Clean Water Act, and recommended modifications to Section 401 to focus state responsibility in water quality certification. 1 SMA therefore appreciates this proposed rulemaking by EPA, which promises to protect water quality and prevent overreach by state co-regulators. We respectfully submit several specific comments for consideration. Request for Certification SMA supports standardizing the contents of a request for water quality certification under 40 CFR 121.5 to improve efficiency, consistency, and predictability. Requiring submission of the federal permit or license application—or a draft of such—and readily available discharge-related water quality information provides sufficient information for certifying authorities to evaluate compliance with applicable water quality requirements. Furthermore, removing the ability of states and tribes to prescribe additional, specific content requirements will reduce inconsistency across jurisdictions and ensure adherence to statutory timelines, lowering administrative burden on permittees without diminishing water quality protection. SMA encourages EPA to clarify that the standardized list of required materials under 40 CFR 121.5 is intended to be sufficient for certification review and that completeness determinations may not be used to expand the scope of review or delay the start of the statutory review period. We request that EPA carefully review use of open-ended language such as “other relevant documentation” in 40 CFR 121.5(c)(4). Timeframe for Certification Analysis and Decision SMA supports EPA’s proposed changes to Section 401 certification timelines, which appropriately reinforce the statutory one-year maximum period for action on a certification request. Firm deadlines are particularly important for steel manufacturers and other permittees undertaking major capital projects subject to certification. Removing automatic extensions tied to public notice or force majeure events promotes timely decision-making. And prohibitions on certifying authority requests for withdrawal will preclude deadline circumvention schemes. Appropriate Scope for Section 401 Certification Review SMA supports proposed changes to the scope of Section 401 certification review in conformance with a plain reading of the Clean Water Act and recent court precedent, limiting such review to a discharge from a federally permitted activity into waters of the United States. Specifically, narrowing 1 America Builds: Clean Water Permitting and Project Delivery Hearing before Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment, 119th Cong. (February 11, 2025) (statement of Noah Hanners, Executive Vice President, Nucor Corporation), available at https://transportation.house.gov/uploadedfiles/02-11-2025_wre_hearing_-_noah_hanners_-_testimony.pdf. review to point-source discharges of an activity—rather than the activity as a whole—will provide clarity for permittees and regulators, and defining “discharge” as being a discharge into waters of the United States aligns with statutory authority and avoids federal overreach into non-jurisdictional waters. These proposed limitations promise to prevent Section 401 certification from being used to regulate aspects of a permitted activity unrelated to jurisdictional water quality impacts from a point source discharge. In response to specific request for comment from EPA regarding narrative water quality standards and the definition of “water quality requirements,” SMA takes the position that reliance on numeric water quality standards alone is more likely to enhance certainty for permittees and therefore would urge EPA to limit the definition accordingly. Similarly, in general SMA supports EPA interpreting the phrase “other appropriate requirement of State law” to constrict the ability of certifying authorities to go outside the bounds of enumerated provisions in the Clean Water Act. Contents of a Certification Decision SMA supports proposed revisions to certification decision documents. The EPA’s approach will promote consistent implementation across all jurisdictions. Clear linkage between conditions and regulatory standards helps permittees understand compliance obligations and avoid conflicting requirements. Requiring certification decisions to cite specific applicable water quality requirements—and removing those requirements pertaining to public notice procedures—will improve transparency and enforceability. Modifications SMA supports proposed revisions to 40 CFR 121.10 clarifying the procedures for modifying a grant of certification; in particular, preventing unilateral modification of grants of certification by certifying authorities. Providing a defined process for applicant involvement in certification modifications promotes transparency, fairness, and better coordination between applicants and certifying authorities. In response to EPA’s specific request for comment on applicant involvement in the development of modifications, SMA notes our support for enhancements to collaboration between applicants and certifying authorities as a general matter. Section 401(a)(2) Process SMA supports EPA’s proposed changes to the Section 401(a)(2) process. Establishing a 90-day deadline for federal agencies to hold a public hearing and make a determination on a state’s objection to issuance of a license or permit will reduce the risk of open-ended delays and allow for predictable resolution of such objections. Furthermore, SMA supports revisions requiring a state to include in an objection a citation to water quality requirements that would be violated by a discharge from a project. Treatment in a Similar Manner as a State SMA supports EPA’s proposed changes that remove the ability of Tribes to obtain treatment in a similar manner as a state for the purposes of Section 401 broadly, and for Section 401(a)(2) specifically. As the agency states, Tribes may obtain this status through existing means to administer water quality standards, a process by which those Tribes may develop rigorous standards that also enhance predictability for applicants in the certification process. Conclusion SMA appreciates the opportunity to provide these comments in response to EPA’s proposed rulemaking updating water quality certification regulations. We support the agency’s efforts to put forward commonsense regulations that encourage economic growth and environmental protection. Sincerely, Brandon Farris Executive Vice President Steel Manufacturers Association
